Article.ArticleTitle: Rise and Shine: The Smoke-free Homes International Network (SHINE).

AffiliationInfo.Affiliation: Institute for Social Marketing and Health, Faculty of Health and Sport Sciences, University of Stirling, Scotland, United Kingdom.
Institute for Social Marketing and Health, Faculty of Health and Sport Sciences, University of Stirling, Scotland, United Kingdom.
Institute for Social Marketing and Health, Faculty of Health and Sport Sciences, University of Stirling, Scotland, United Kingdom.
Tobacco Control Unit, Catalan Institute of Oncology - WHO Collaborating Centre for Tobacco Control, l'Hospitalet de Llobregat, Catalonia, Spain.
Centre for Biomedical Research in Respiratory Diseases, Institute of Health Carlos III, Madrid, Spain.
Tobacco Control Research Group, Bellvitge Biomedical Research Institute, l'Hospitalet de Llobregat, Catalonia, Spain.
Department of Clinical Sciences, Faculty of Medicine and Health Sciences, University of Barcelona, Barcelona, Spain.
Tobacco Control Unit, Catalan Institute of Oncology - WHO Collaborating Centre for Tobacco Control, l'Hospitalet de Llobregat, Catalonia, Spain.
Centre for Biomedical Research in Respiratory Diseases, Institute of Health Carlos III, Madrid, Spain.
Tobacco Control Research Group, Bellvitge Biomedical Research Institute, l'Hospitalet de Llobregat, Catalonia, Spain.
Department of Clinical Sciences, Faculty of Medicine and Health Sciences, University of Barcelona, Barcelona, Spain.

Institute for Social Marketing and Health, Faculty of Health and Sport Sciences, University of Stirling, Scotland, United Kingdom.

MedlineCitation.CoiStatement: The authors have each completed and submitted an ICMJE form for Disclosure of Potential Conflicts of Interest. The authors declare that they have no competing interests, financial or otherwise, related to the current work. E. Fernández states that he received a competitive research grant for a SFH project from Fondo de Investigación Sanitaria, Instituto de Salud Carlos III, Ministry of Universities of Spain. R. Howell states that she was a Co-Investigator for two projects, one for the Scottish Government (2021-2022) and another for the Chief Scientist Office (2023-2026), and all grants were paid to the University of Stirling. R. O’Donnell states that she was the Principal Investigator in projects for the Scottish Government (2021-2022), the Chief Scientist Office (2023-2026), and the University of Stirling Global Engagement Research Fund, resources provided by the Scottish Funding Council (2023-2024, and 2024-2025). She also states that she was Co-Investigator in projects funded by the Newton Fund (2020-2022), and Medical Research Council (2022-2024), and the Economic and Social Research Council (ESRC) (2021-2022). All grants were paid to the University of Stirling. The author also states that consulting fees were paid to the University of Stirling from the Institute of Public Health in Ireland, for a review of progress on reducing SHS exposure in Northern Ireland (2023-2024, and 2024-2025). She also had a leadership/fiduciary role without payment in ASH Scotland Short-Life Working Group on Smoke-Free Spaces (2024-2025) and the Scottish Government Tobacco Action Plan Working Group (2023-2024). S. Semple states that he was Principal Investigator in projects funded by the Newton Fund (2020-2022) and the Medical Research Council (2022-2024). He also states that he was Co-Investigator on projects funded by the Chief Scientist Office (2023-2026), and in the University of Stirling Global Engagement Research Fund, resources provided by the Scottish Funding Council (2023-2024, and 2024-2025). All grants were paid to the University of Stirling. The author also states that consulting fees were paid to the University from the Institute of Public Health in Ireland, for a review of progress on reducing SHS exposure in Northern Ireland (2023-2024, and 2024-2025). The author had a leadership/fiduciary role without payment in the ASH Scotland Short-Life Working Group on Smoke-Free Spaces (2024-2025) and the Scottish Government Tobacco Action Plan Working Group (2023-2024). P. Teodorowski states that he was Co-Investigator in a project funded by the University of Stirling Global Engagement Research Fund, resources provided by the Scottish Funding Council (2023-2024). The grant was paid to the University.

Reference.Citation: World Health Organization . Smokefree homes factsheet for policy makers. WHO; 2023. Accessed March 13, 2025. https://www.who.int/docs/librariesprovider2/default-document-library/smokefree-homes-fs-policy-makers.pdf?sfvrsn=6624b74e_3&download=true

World Health Organization . WHO report on the global tobacco epidemic, 2023: Protect people from second-hand smoke. WHO; 2023. Accessed March 13, 2025. https://iris.who.int/bitstream/handle/10665/372043/9789240077164-eng.pdf?sequence=1

Zhai C, Hu D, Yu G, et al. Global, regional, and national deaths, disability-adjusted life years, years lived with disability, and years of life lost for the global disease burden attributable to second-hand smoke, 1990-2019: A systematic analysis for the Global Burden of Disease Study. Sci Total Environ. 2023;862:160677. doi: 10.1016/j.scitotenv.2022.160677
Carreras G, Lugo A, Gallus S, et al. Burden of disease attributable to second-hand smoke exposure: A systematic review. Prev Med. 2019;129:105833. doi: 10.1016/j.ypmed.2019.105833

Tigova O, Stival C, Castellano Y, et al. Prevalence and determinants of smoke-free homes in 12 European countries: the TackSHS Survey. ERJ Open Res. 2025;11(2):00950-2024. doi: 10.1183/23120541.00950-2024

Henderson E, Rodriguez Guerrero LA, Continente X, et al. Measurement of airborne nicotine, as a marker of secondhand smoke exposure, in homes with residents who smoke in 9 European countries. Environ Res. 2023;219:115118. doi: 10.1016/j.envres.2022.115118

Kuntz B, Lampert T. Social disparities in parental smoking and young children’s exposure to secondhand smoke at home: a time-trend analysis of repeated cross-sectional data from the German KiGGS study between 2003-2006 and 2009-2012. BMC Public Health. 2016;16:485. doi: 10.1186/s12889-016-3175-x

Hajizadeh M, Nandi A. The socioeconomic gradient of secondhand smoke exposure in children: evidence from 26 low-income and middle-income countries. Tob Control. 2016;25(e2):e146-e155. doi: 10.1136/tobaccocontrol-2015-052828
Semple S, Latif N. How long does secondhand smoke remain in household air: analysis of PM2.5 data from smokers’ homes. Nicotine Tob Res. 2014;16(10):1365-1370. doi: 10.1093/ntr/ntu089

Semple S, Apsley A, Azmina Ibrahim T, Turner SW, Cherrie JW. Fine particulate matter concentrations in smoking households: just how much secondhand smoke do you breathe in if you live with a smoker who smokes indoors? Tob Control. 2015;24(e3):e205-e211. doi: 10.1136/tobaccocontrol-2014-051635
Seo DC, Torabi MR, Weaver AE. Factors influencing openness to future smoking among nonsmoking adolescents. J Sch Health. 2008;78(6):328-358. doi: 10.1111/j.1746-1561.2008.00310.x
UNICEF . A summary of the UN Convention on the Rights of the Child. UNICEF; 2019. Accessed March 7, 2025. https://www.unicef.org.uk/wp-content/uploads/2019/10/UNCRC_summary-1_1.pdf

Semple S, Tigova O, Howell R, et al. Protecting children from second-hand tobacco smoke in the home: the need for a new approach. Tob Control. Published online March 15, 2025. doi: 10.1136/tc-2024-059205
